title: Moroccan Vegetable Tagine

overview.description: A warmly spiced Moroccan vegetable stew with chickpeas, root vegetables, tomatoes and dried fruit, slowly cooked until tender.

ingredients: 1 small butternut squash, peeled and cut into chunks
1 x 400g tin chickpeas, drained and rinsed
1 preserved lemon, flesh removed and rind finely chopped, optional
method: Heat the olive oil in a large heavy-based casserole or tagine over medium heat.
Add the onion and cook until softened.
Add the garlic and fresh ginger.
Add the cumin, ground coriander, turmeric, paprika, cinnamon, ground ginger and black pepper.
Add the carrot, potatoes and butternut squash.
Stir to coat the vegetables in the spices.
Add the chopped tomatoes and vegetable stock.
Add the chickpeas and dried apricots.
Add the preserved lemon if using.
Cover and cook for 25–30 minutes until the root vegetables are beginning to soften.
Continue cooking uncovered or partially covered until all the vegetables are tender and the sauce has thickened.
Finish with the fresh coriander and parsley.

learning.did-you-know: A tagine refers both to the distinctive conical cooking vessel and to the slow-cooked dish prepared in it. The shape helps condensation return to the food during cooking.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to build a warmly spiced North African stew and how sweet, savoury and acidic ingredients are balanced in Moroccan cooking.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A cheap and highly adaptable Pantry Monday meal that uses chickpeas, root vegetables and store-cupboard spices to create a filling dish with a completely different flavour profile from the Indian vegetable curries.
chapman-tips: Add the harder vegetables before the softer ones so everything finishes at the same time.
Cut the vegetables into fairly large pieces so they retain their shape during cooking.
Dried apricots provide sweetness and are a classic pairing with Moroccan spices.
Preserved lemon is optional but adds a distinctive salty, citrusy flavour.
Don't drown the tagine in liquid; the finished sauce should be thick and cling to the vegetables.
Harissa can be served separately so the children can enjoy the dish without excessive heat.
The tagine is often even better the next day as the flavours develop.
You can substitute swede, sweet potato, pumpkin or other seasonal vegetables.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Allow to cool completely before freezing.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently on the hob or in the microwave. Serve with couscous, rice or bread.
